content.full_text:
A-82-159

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901          322-6441              322-6444

September 14, 1982


Jerry Jordan
California Municipal Utilities
Association
1213 K Street, Suite 103
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Letter of
     September 3, 1982

Dear Mr. Jordan:

     Thank you for your letter requesting clarification of my
advice letter concerning a legislator's retirement dinner.  In
that letter, I did not discuss the application of the $10 gift
prohibition for lobbyists (Government Code Section 86203) since
the issue was not raised.  However, I did note that a lobbyist
could not buy more than five tickets to the retirement dinner
without being in violation of this prohibition ($2 from each
ticket was a gift to the legislator).

     This advice was not contrary to previous staff advice on the
subject.  If a lobbyist's only participation in an event such as
a legislator's retirement dinner is to purchase a ticket or
tickets and to attend the event, it is not prohibited under
Government Code Section 86203 so long as the price paid for the
tickets includes at most $10 that could be used to make a gift
benefiting the legislator.  However, a lobbyist may not
participate in the arranging of the event which includes a
common plan to provide a benefit to the retiring legislator
which exceeds $10 in value.

     I have enclosed a copy of a staff advice letter to
Assemblyman John Knox (A-78-05-009, May 19, 1978) which contains
this exact advice.

Jerry Jordan
September 14, 1982
Page 2


     I hope this discussion has been helpful to you.  If you
would like to discuss this further, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diana Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF;plh

CALIFORNIA MUNICIPAL

1213 K STREET, SUITE 103 • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 • (916) 441-1733

JERRY JORDAN, Executive Director

Turlock Irrigation District

Metropolitan Water District

BOARD OF GOVERNORS

EDWARD K. AGHJAYAN
Palo Alto

H. LES BROOKS
Modesto Irrigation District

DEAN W. COFFEY
Hetch Hetchy Power
San Francisco

THOMAS J. DOSH
Stockton

KENNETH D. DUKE
Inglewood

GORDON W. HOIT
Anaheim

RICHARD T.W. KING
San Diego

JOHN W. LINDBLAD
Napa

NORMAN E NICHOLS
Los Angeles

ROBERT J. O'DONNELL
Long Beach

EVERETT C. ROSS
Riverside

WILLIAM C. WALBRIDGE
Sacramento Municipal Utility District

HERBERT B. WESSEL
San Bernardino

ADVISORY COUNCIL

IVAN L. BATEMAN
Los Angeles

E. K. DAVIS
Sacramento

CLYDE N. MOORE
Long Beach

CARL I. RICHEY
Sacramento

R. A. SKINNER
Los Angeles

September 3, 1982

Ms. Diane Fishburn
Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O Box 807
Sacramento, Ca 95804

Dear Ms. Fishburn:

I read with interest a report on your advice on gifts to
retiring legislators. It appears to be inconsistent with report
requirements of lobbyists.

You have apparently said that lobbyists can contribute up to
$10 each per month to a gift which will total more than $10. The
FPPC staff, however, has consistently advised lobbyists that when
contributing to the cost of retirement parties the total amount spent
cannot exceed a total of $10 per person and that each lobbyist must
report the entire expenditure regardless of the share actually paid by
the lobbyist. I believe these two pieces of advice to be in direct
conflict.

I would appreciate your clarification of this contradiction.

Sincerely,

Jerry Jordan
Jerry Jordan

Jrjael

An organization for the protection of municipally owned utilities.

C.M.U.A. members provide utility services to more than 70% of the people of California

sections.conclusion: If a lobbyist's only participation in an event such as a legislator's retirement dinner is to purchase a ticket or tickets and to attend the event, it is not prohibited under Government Code Section 86203 so long as the price paid for the tickets includes at most $10 that could be used to make a gift benefitting the legislator. However, a lobbyist may not participate in the arranging of the event which includes a common plan to provide a benefit to the retiring legislator which exceeds $10 in value.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether FPPC advice on the $10 gift prohibition for lobbyists under Government Code Section 86203, specifically regarding lobbyist participation in and contributions to a legislator's retirement dinner, is consistent with lobbyist reporting requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Lobbyists may purchase and attend retirement dinner tickets if the gift component does not exceed $10, but cannot participate in arranging events designed to collectively provide gifts exceeding $10 to the legislator. This advice is consistent with prior FPPC staff guidance.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (September 3, 1982) and FPPC response (September 14, 1982). No explicit QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but clear question and answer structure evident. Question reconstructed from incoming letter content. Confidence reduced due to lack of formal section headers and some ambiguity in section boundaries.

QUESTION: Whether FPPC advice on the $10 gift prohibition for lobbyists under Government Code Section 86203, specifically regarding lobbyist participation in and contributions to a legislator's retirement dinner, is consistent with lobbyist reporting requirements.

CONCLUSION: If a lobbyist's only participation in an event such as a legislator's retirement dinner is to purchase a ticket or tickets and to attend the event, it is not prohibited under Government Code Section 86203 so long as the price paid for the tickets includes at most $10 that could be used to make a gift benefitting the legislator. However, a lobbyist may not participate in the arranging of the event which includes a common plan to provide a benefit to the retiring legislator which exceeds $10 in value.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Maurai Fishburn responds to the California Municipal Utilities Association's inquiry clarifying that lobbyists may purchase retirement dinner tickets up to $10 in gift value per transaction without violating the gift prohibition, but may not participate in organizing events that collectively exceed the $10 limit.
